title: Sub vs Dub Stuff Kurikaeshi eien ni
display_title: Sub vs Dub Stuff Kurikaeshi eien ni
wikitext:
[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Jun 07, 2011 10:32 am I'll just be a little more detailed in what I said here . On a reason purely based on my personal background, I don't see an actual reason for a dub unless someone has poor eye-sight. (And I've met people who watch dubs for that reason.) I watch films from many different countries that use many different forms of visual motion picture (animation or live-action) to express themselves, and I've always seen them in their original languages. You lose half of the actors' performances, and therefore half of the reason why the director casted those actors, if you replace their vocal inflections with those of other people the director did not intend to cast. Just watch the Spanish dub of That Dark Knight and watch how drastically un-Heath Ledger-esque the Joker suddenly sounded, and therefore not at all what director Christopher Nolan intended. And would you honestly think the character of Darth Vader or Mufasa would be half as interesting if the characters were dubbed by someone other than James Earl Jones? Therefore, I see no reason why I should have to listen to a bunch of actors that were never intended to be in the film suddenly think they can do better than the actor the actual director casted, despite the fact that the original director never casted them and is not presently directing them. This issue also leads into voice inflection, because not only is the actor not what the director intended, but the actor is portraying the character in a way that the director never intended, or the actor is executing the character in a way less stellar than the director would have achieved with the actors he himself had casted and directed. This is one of the main reasons why I watch the Subs over the Dubs. The dub actor often times lacks the character subtext present in the original performances. A good example of this would be most of Tiffany Grant's lines as Asuka in either Evangelion franchise. Both characters sound annoying, yes. Both characters are supposed to sound this way. But Grant's performance never really accomplished the finer points of the underlying reasons why Asuka behaved that way, whereas Yuko Miyamura directed by Hideaki Anno himself, and was explained the underlying turmoil of the character very early on in the production. Grant doesn't pull this off until almost the close of the series, and by that time it sounds somewhat contrived, almost as if she wasn't told until she came in the recording booth that day that the character was much more complicated than she had previously thought she was. (Because that's exactly what happened.) An interesting study in this is a phone conversation between Asuka and Misato in Evangelion 2.22: You Can (Not) Advance . Listen to both Grant's version and Yuko's version of Asuka's last dialogue scene in the film. The words that are spoken are almost the same within terms of context and meaning, but the inflection is much different. Yuko's Asuka sounds more interested in the new developments in her social life, almost as if she was pleasantly blind-sided by it. Whereas Grant's version sounds more like Asuka is still much too better for people like Shinji, and she's wondering why she's even putting up with him. Again, the meaning and context of the words the actors are speaking are the same, but the different inflections actors brought to it put a whole different spin on the dialogue. (I've seen those people with poor eye-sight I mentioned earlier strain to see the subs for particular animes for this very reason.) One would think that the issue really couldn't be fixed by having Hideaki Anno come over and direct Tiffany Grant or Spike Spencer because it's obvious from the "Bad English Dub" opening of the Japanese dub of 2.22 that the language barrier would become too much of an issue in communication to those actors. But one has to remember that the English voices in the Japanese dub for Episode 18 of the original Neon Genesis Evangelion TV series was nearly flawless, and those most likely had Anno supervising the direction of those recordings. One has to wonder if the "Bad English Dub" of 2.22 wasn't an intentional plug by Anno commenting on the poor English dubs of Japanese animation back in the late 80's that later on evolved into the English dubs we have today. One of the few exceptions I give is to Cowboy Bebop because most of the sub-text of the over-arcing show is how cool Western Culture can be. And since those of us living in America know it best, who better than our actors to portray that? And even then Spike's English dub actor was able to handle the underlying sub-text of the character himself relatively well. But these are exceptions I give strictly based off of cultural differences, and even then I watch the original Japanese dub just about as often and the English dub. But, whether you like the original series or the new movies, Evangelion is a very Japanese franchise. And it only makes sense that Japanese people sound like, well, Japanese people.

[]:             Quote Post by BornIn1142 » Tue Jun 07, 2011 10:39 am FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: One has to wonder if the "Bad English Dub" of 2.22 wasn't an intentional plug by Anno commenting on the poor English dubs of Japanese animation back in the late 80's that later on evolved into the English dubs we have today.

[]:             Quote Post by Tarnsman » Tue Jun 07, 2011 10:46 am FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Asuka's last dialogue scene in Evangelion 2.22: You Can (Not) Advance . The words that are spoken are almost the same within terms of context and meaning, but the inflection is much different. Yuko's Asuka sounds more interested in the new developments in her social life, almost as if she was pleasantly blind-sided by it. Whereas Grant's version sounds more like Asuka is still much too better for people like Shinji, and she's wondering why she's even putting up with him. Again, the meaning and context of the words the actors are speaking are the same, but the different inflections actors brought to it put a whole different spin on the dialogue.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Jun 07, 2011 11:44 am BornIn1142 wrote: ...Are you serious? No, really, is this a serious proposition that you are making for real ?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Jun 07, 2011 11:55 am Can we actually stick to the original topic? (For those who can't, don't be surprised if your posts get baleeted.) I'm not sure I've ever seen such a list, myself.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Jun 07, 2011 12:03 pm Reichu wrote: Can we actually stick to the original topic? (For those who can't, don't be surprised if your posts get baleeted.)

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Jun 07, 2011 1:12 pm Tarnsman wrote: I would have to disagree. Asuka's supposed to sound fake, like she's trying to act disinterested in having a social life despite really enjoying her new "friends", which I think both Yuko and Grant sound.

[]:             Quote Post by BornIn1142 » Tue Jun 07, 2011 1:33 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: It's also obvious that English was the native language for the actors playing the roles of those English-speaking characters in the Japanese dub. (Except for Kaji and Mari, obviously.) They must have heard what was coming out of their mouths and thought it sounded silly. So, I'd even suggest that those involved went the extra mile in the complete opposite direction of making everything sound natural and believable.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Jun 07, 2011 1:41 pm I'd agree with all of that if those issues were also mirrored in Episode 18 of Neon Genesis Evangelion . But they're not. so something has to be different about this one, possibly intentionally. But I do agree with their reasons for keeping the original Kaji actor. It would have been nice to get a sound-alike just for those two or three lines, but probably wasn't possible.

[]:             Quote Post by BornIn1142 » Tue Jun 07, 2011 1:45 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: I'd agree with all of that if those issues were also mirrored in Episode 18 of Neon Genesis Evangelion . But they're not. so something has to be different about this one, possibly intentionally.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Jun 07, 2011 1:47 pm BornIn1142 wrote: I don't even understand the issue here. Of course it's easier to hire a random extra who speaks English than to deal with an existing actor who just doesn't. Koichi Yamadera apparently doesn't, and the directors had to deal with that. Bagheera's suggestion to just replace him was obviously unacceptable for them, and I can understand why.

[]:             Quote Post by BornIn1142 » Tue Jun 07, 2011 1:49 pm Bagheera wrote: That wasn't really my suggestion. My suggestion was to cast someone who could speak English as Kaji if English-speaking scenes were required for the part or, failing that, to avoid scenes the VAs weren't equipped to handle. Hiring someone else to do the lines is sort of a last ditch effort, but it's not like the results could have come out much worse.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Jun 07, 2011 1:55 pm Well, I was referring more to the native English speakers sounding so silly, not the native Japanese speakers sounding odd speaking in a language foreign to them. The original Japanese cast can have their characters, no matter what language they try to speak. As for my reference to Episode 18, that episode opened with two or three native English-speaking voice actors reading lines, and they sounded completely natural in their speech rather than a bad 80's English anime dub over. I figured if Anno was gonna screw up a native English-speaking person, he would have done it back when he was younger, more limited in time and budget, and less experienced than he is now with the bigger-budget, longer scheduled, more experienced crew he's working with. My point is, with these new movies, there should be fewer obstacles to him getting native English-speaking actors to sound natural, so why do they sound sillier in this big-budget film than they did back in Episode 18 of the original TV series? My theory is because Anno probably intended it. It's about as absurd as, say, EoE's acronym being seen in the film itself. (Haha! Smugleaf, I almost miss you.)

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Tue Jun 07, 2011 2:05 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: My point is, with these new movies, there should be fewer obstacles to him getting native English-speaking actors to sound natural, so why do they sound sillier in this big-budget film than they did back in Episode 18 of the original TV series? My theory is because Anno probably intended it.

[]:             Quote Post by Tarnsman » Tue Jun 07, 2011 2:19 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: That's interesting. I wonder is this difference of opinion of Asuka's mood during that scene also effects the differences of opinion that people have expressed on this forum over Asuka's character development throughout 2.22 .

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Jun 07, 2011 4:06 pm BornIn1142 wrote: Personally, I think it would have been lot more jarring if Kaji's performance was split between two actors than it was for us to get some bad English.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Tue Jun 07, 2011 5:30 pm I'ma copy/pasta some stuff regarding sub/dub in locked topic: Bagheera wrote: @The dub/sub debate: There's really no comparison here. Apart from the (valid) points FFF4E gave above, the performances in the English version really aren't that good. I always kinda have to laugh when someone goes on about how good Asuka or Misato are in the dub, because...well, they aren't. They're just overwrought and emotional and painful to listen to. (as a disclaimer, I've highly critical of acting in general; even most professional actors don't do a very good job of it. This is just exaggerated in the case of U.S. VAs because it's still not a serious market and the people involved usually don't have that much training when it comes to making it work. It's far better than it was a couple of decades ago, but that's not saying much.) @Literal subs: Yes, these are also painful. But the subs for NGE/EoE are not literal, so the point is kinda irrelevant. You're getting an adaptation whether you go with the sub or the dub; the sub version just tends to be more true to the original source material. The only downside to subs is the fact that NGE is such a rich visual story; there's a lot going on with how shots are framed, voices and other things in the background, and so on and so forth that adds immensely to understanding the story. However, I find that this is the sort of thing the demands frequent pausing and rewinding anyway, so you don't get that much more out of it by watching the dub (arguably less, actually, since it's easier to keep track of multiple subtitle tracks than it is to make out voices when people are talking over one another).

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Jun 07, 2011 5:54 pm esselfortium wrote: This rationalization is getting rather convoluted, I think. It seems easier to just assume Anno isn't infallible in His dubbing techniques.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Tue Jun 07, 2011 6:24 pm Stryker wrote: @1st quote: What you consider "decent/good performances in Anime and film" will not help unless: 1. We know what they are. 2. We had more then just you who watched them on this forum.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Tue Jun 07, 2011 8:05 pm Even I'm getting sick of this debate. If Warren Peace doesn't show up sometime soon I may just swear off Eva -related sub vs. dub arguments forever. @Reichu re: "Dub Yojimbo": God, that's a scary thought. If I were to stand for anything, I would hope it'd be something less titanically trivial. Maybe artless sophistries. For clarification, my position (such as it may be called) is NOT that the dub is perfect, and certainly not that it is superior to the Japanese track. Rather, 1) I personally enjoy it (at least in part by virtue of nostalgia), 2) I am aware that it is immensely flawed, 3) I think it is decent on its own merits and its alleged awfulness tends to be widely overstated, 4) I do not believe that watching a dub is an illegitimate means of experiencing a work of art or entertainment, especially animation, and 5) I think there are a few specific things that the Eva dub and its cast did not just decently, but exceptionally. There. Anyway, please carry on with this inherently circular debate.
